title: The Rich Fool and His Barns
subtitle: A man begs Christ to divide his inheritance, and a rich man dreams of greater barns

stillness: Be still, and turn from the noise of having and holding.
Here the Lord refuses to be a judge of quarrels.
Sit in silence, and let Him examine the intention of your heart.
gospel: Someone from the crowd said to Him:
"Master, tell my brother to divide the inheritance with me."
"Man, who has appointed me a judge, or a divider, over you?"
"Take heed, and beware of all covetousness;
for a man's life does not consist in the abundance of the things he possesses."
And He told them a parable:
"The field of a certain rich man brought forth plentiful fruits.
And he thought within himself, saying:
'What shall I do, for I have no room to gather my fruits?'
And he said: 'This will I do:
I will pull down my barns, and build greater.'"
behold: Consider this man of the crowd, who knew Jesus to be just,
yet asked an unfitting thing, for it was neither the time nor the place.
He had never tasted how good and how pleasant it is for brothers to dwell as one.
It was not law that drew him, but the love of having.
Now look upon the rich man whose field gave fruit in abundance.
See him thinking within himself, not speaking aloud, lest he be heard:
for the greedy rich man fears every living soul.
He sees a rich man, and reckons him a robber;
he sees a poor man, and suspects a thief.
He was made anxious by his plenty, unhappy in the goods he had,
and more unhappy in the goods to come.
The field brought him not so much a harvest as heavier groans.
consider: Covetousness is the common vice: of clergy and laity, of religious and secular, of the small and the great.
It is the root of all evils, and it separates the soul from God.
Beware of ALL covetousness, says the Lord, the great and the small alike,
for the inward Judge discerns by what intention each thing is done.
This rich man did not need to enlarge his barns.
Good barns already stood prepared for him: the bellies of the hungry poor.
But he did not remember our common nature.
He knew how to think only of hoarding here, never of the heavenly granary,
whose barns are the poor of Christ.
He should have said: I will scatter, giving to the poor.
He calls the goods his own, and in this he speaks a lie,
for 'mine' and 'thine' were spoken out of iniquity,
since by the law of nature all things were common.
These are not the goods of a man, which he cannot carry away with him.
Is it certain you will reach the old age for whose sake you hoard?
prayer: Lord Jesus, You who are not the God of division, but of gathering, of peace, and of unity,
You who came to make men of one heart and one soul, that they might hold all things in common:
turn me away from the earthly, for whose sake You stooped to descend.
Let me not reckon mine the goods I cannot carry away with me.
Teach me that a man's possession is not earthly, but heavenly,
and that the true riches of a man are spiritual, not of the earth.
Open in me the barns that are the poor of Christ:
that I may not hoard, but scatter and give. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Non oportebat eum horrea dilatare, bene erant horrea parata, scilicet pauperum esurientium ventres.
latinJewel.english: He had no need to enlarge his barns; good barns already stood prepared for him — namely, the bellies of the hungry poor.
